_meta.description: Comprehensive financial knowledge base — canonical definitions, formulas, and conventions compiled from Investopedia, Corporate Finance Institute, Wall Street Prep, Investing.com, and SEC filings standards.

quick_ratio.name: Quick Ratio (Acid-Test Ratio)
quick_ratio.definition: Measures a company's ability to meet short-term obligations using only its most liquid assets, excluding inventory.
quick_ratio.formula: Quick Ratio = (Total Current Assets - Inventories - Prepaid Expenses) / Total Current Liabilities
quick_ratio.alternate_formula: Quick Ratio = (Cash & Cash Equivalents + Short-Term Marketable Securities + Accounts Receivable) / Total Current Liabilities
conventions: EXCLUDES inventory from current assets — this is the key difference from the current ratio.
EXCLUDES prepaid expenses and other non-liquid current assets.
Use balance sheet values as of period end (not averages).
Short-term investments / marketable securities should be included if they are liquid.

A quick ratio ≥ 1.0 is generally considered healthy; < 1.0 means the company CANNOT fully cover current liabilities with liquid assets — this is definitively NOT healthy and needs improvement. Do NOT hedge or say 'approximately healthy' for ratios below 1.0. A ratio of 0.96 is NOT healthy.
PREFERRED formula: Quick Ratio = (Total Current Assets - Inventories - Prepaid Expenses) / Total Current Liabilities. This is simpler and avoids cherry-picking which receivables to include.
When COMPARING quick ratios across periods: state the direction clearly ('improved' or 'declined') with BOTH values and the percentage change.
quick_ratio.where_to_find: Balance sheet — Current Assets section and Current Liabilities section

current_ratio.name: Current Ratio
current_ratio.definition: Measures a company's ability to pay short-term obligations due within one year.
current_ratio.formula: Current Ratio = Total Current Assets / Total Current Liabilities

numerator: Total current assets (cash, receivables, inventory, prepaid expenses, etc.)

conventions: INCLUDES inventory, unlike the quick ratio.
Use balance sheet values as of period end.
A ratio between 1.0 and 2.0 is generally considered acceptable.
Above 2.0 may indicate inefficient use of assets.
current_ratio.where_to_find: Balance sheet — Total Current Assets and Total Current Liabilities line items

working_capital.name: Working Capital (Net Working Capital)
working_capital.definition: The difference between a company's current assets and current liabilities, representing short-term financial health.
working_capital.formula: Working Capital = Total Current Assets - Total Current Liabilities
conventions: Use TOTAL current assets and TOTAL current liabilities from the balance sheet — these are the line items explicitly labeled 'Total current assets' and 'Total current liabilities'. However, if the balance sheet shows a separate 'Other current assets' or 'Other current liabilities' line that is large and unexplained, consider noting both the total and the operating-only figures.
Positive working capital = company can cover short-term obligations.
Negative working capital = current liabilities exceed current assets.

For fintech/payment companies (e.g. PayPal, Square): customer funds and pass-through balances ARE included in standard working capital. Only exclude them if the question asks for 'operating working capital' specifically.
If the gold answer says 'considers only operating current assets', the question is asking for OPERATING working capital. But by default, use the TOTAL line items from the balance sheet.
Do NOT subtract non-current items.
IMPORTANT: Report the exact dollar amount you compute. Common values should be in millions or billions matching the filing's units.
working_capital.where_to_find: Balance sheet — Total Current Assets minus Total Current Liabilities

return_on_equity.name: Return on Equity (ROE)
return_on_equity.definition: Measures a company's profitability relative to shareholders' equity.
return_on_equity.formula: ROE = Net Income / Average Shareholders' Equity
return_on_equity.alternate_formula: ROE = Net Income / Shareholders' Equity (end of period)

numerator: Net income attributable to common shareholders (from income statement)
denominator: Average total shareholders' equity = (Beginning Equity + Ending Equity) / 2

conventions: Use net income attributable to the parent company / common shareholders, NOT total net income if there are non-controlling interests.
Prefer average shareholders' equity over end-of-period for more accurate results.
If only one period of equity is available, use end-of-period.
ROE of 15-20% is generally considered good.
DuPont decomposition: ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier.
return_on_equity.where_to_find: Income statement (net income) and Balance sheet (shareholders' equity)

return_on_assets.name: Return on Assets (ROA)
return_on_assets.definition: Measures how efficiently a company uses its assets to generate profit.
return_on_assets.formula: ROA = Net Income / Total Assets (end of period)
return_on_assets.alternate_formula: ROA = Net Income / Average Total Assets (when question explicitly asks for average)

numerator: Net income attributable to the parent company / common shareholders — NOT total net income if non-controlling interests exist
denominator: Total assets from the balance sheet as of period end

conventions: CRITICAL: Use net income attributable to the parent company / common shareholders. If the income statement shows both 'Net income' and 'Net income attributable to [Company]', use the LATTER.
PREFERRED: Use END-OF-PERIOD total assets from the CURRENT year's balance sheet only. Do NOT average with prior year unless explicitly asked.
CRITICAL: If the question says 'based on the statement of financial position' or 'balance sheet', this means a SINGLE period's data — use end-of-period, NOT average.
ROA varies widely by industry — capital-intensive industries will have lower ROA.
Express as a decimal (e.g., 0.05) or percentage (e.g., 5%) matching the question's format. If the question says 'round to the nearest two decimal places', give exactly that precision.
return_on_assets.where_to_find: Income statement (net income attributable to company) and Balance sheet (total assets)

operating_margin.name: Operating Margin (Operating Profit Margin)
operating_margin.definition: The percentage of revenue remaining after deducting operating expenses.

numerator: Operating income (also called EBIT or operating earnings)

conventions: Operating income = Revenue - COGS - Operating Expenses (SG&A, R&D, D&A).
Operating income is BEFORE interest and taxes.
Use the operating income line from the income statement.
When comparing periods, compute margin for each period and take the difference.
operating_margin.where_to_find: Income statement — Operating Income and Total Revenue / Net Sales

gross_profit_margin.name: Gross Profit Margin
gross_profit_margin.definition: The percentage of revenue remaining after subtracting the cost of goods sold.

numerator: Gross profit = Revenue - Cost of Goods Sold

conventions: COGS includes direct production costs (materials, direct labor, manufacturing overhead).
COGS does NOT include SG&A, R&D, or depreciation (unless directly tied to production).
Typical ranges: 5-10% for grocery/retail, 40-80% for software/tech.
gross_profit_margin.where_to_find: Income statement — Revenue and Cost of Goods Sold / Cost of Revenue

net_profit_margin.name: Net Profit Margin
net_profit_margin.definition: The percentage of revenue remaining as net income after all expenses.
net_profit_margin.formula: Net Profit Margin = (Net Income / Total Revenue) × 100

numerator: Net income (bottom line of income statement)

conventions: Net income is AFTER all expenses including COGS, operating expenses, interest, taxes, and other items.
Use net income attributable to the company, not including non-controlling interests.
net_profit_margin.where_to_find: Income statement — Net Income and Total Revenue

ebitda.name: EBITDA
aliases: earnings before interest taxes depreciation amortization
ebitda.definition: Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization — a proxy for operating cash flow.
ebitda.alternate_formula: EBITDA = Net Income + Interest Expense + Income Tax Expense + Depreciation + Amortization

from_cash_flow: Depreciation and amortization of property, equipment and intangibles

conventions: EBITDA is a NON-GAAP metric.
Unadjusted EBITDA uses the formula above without further adjustments.

CRITICAL — D&A definition: 'Depreciation and Amortization' in EBITDA means ONLY depreciation of property, plant & equipment (PP&E) and amortization of intangible assets. Do NOT include amortization of content assets (streaming libraries, film costs), amortization of financing costs, or amortization of right-of-use / lease assets. These are operating expenses, not D&A for EBITDA purposes.

On the cash flow statement, the correct line is typically 'Depreciation and amortization of property and equipment' or 'Depreciation and amortization' (the small number, not the large content amortization figures).
When the question asks for 'unadjusted' EBITDA, use only the standard formula with PP&E D&A.
Adjusted EBITDA may exclude one-time charges, stock-based compensation, restructuring costs.
ebitda.where_to_find: Calculated from Income Statement (Operating Income) and Cash Flow Statement (D&A of PP&E/intangibles line)

ebitda_margin.name: EBITDA Margin
ebitda_margin.definition: EBITDA expressed as a percentage of total revenue.
conventions: Unadjusted EBITDA Margin uses standard EBITDA (EBIT + D&A) divided by revenue.
For multi-year average EBITDA margin: calculate EBITDA margin for each year, then average.
ebitda_margin.where_to_find: Derived from Income Statement and Cash Flow Statement

debt_to_equity.name: Debt-to-Equity Ratio (D/E)
debt_to_equity.definition: Measures the proportion of a company's funding that comes from debt versus equity.
debt_to_equity.formula: D/E Ratio = Total Liabilities / Total Shareholders' Equity
debt_to_equity.alternate_formula: D/E Ratio = Total Debt / Total Shareholders' Equity (narrower, uses only interest-bearing debt)
conventions: The broader formula uses total liabilities (most common in standard analysis).
Some analysts use only long-term debt or total interest-bearing debt in the numerator.
D/E ≤ 1.5 is generally desirable; varies significantly by industry.
Financial institutions typically have much higher D/E ratios.
debt_to_equity.where_to_find: Balance sheet — Total Liabilities and Total Shareholders' Equity

inventory_turnover.name: Inventory Turnover Ratio
inventory_turnover.definition: Measures how many times a company sells and replaces its inventory over a period.
inventory_turnover.formula: Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory
inventory_turnover.alternate_formula: Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold / Ending Inventory (simpler, when only one period available)

numerator: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) from income statement — use 'Cost of products sold', 'Cost of Sales', or 'Cost of Revenue' line
denominator: Average Inventory = (Beginning Inventory + Ending Inventory) / 2, where Beginning Inventory = Ending Inventory from prior year's balance sheet

conventions: Use COGS (or 'Cost of products sold' or 'Cost of Sales'), NOT revenue, in the numerator.
PREFERRED: use AVERAGE inventory = (Beginning of year + End of year) / 2. Beginning of year inventory = prior year's ending inventory from the comparative balance sheet.
If only one period's inventory is available, use ending inventory.
Round to one decimal place unless the question specifies otherwise.
When the question says 'how many times has [company] sold its inventory', this IS the inventory turnover ratio.
Higher turnover = more efficient inventory management.
For utility/energy companies, inventory is mostly fuel and spare parts — note that conventional inventory metrics may have limited relevance.
inventory_turnover.where_to_find: Income statement (COGS / Cost of Sales / Cost of products sold) and Balance sheet (Inventory — current year AND prior year for average)

days_payable_outstanding.name: Days Payable Outstanding (DPO)
days_payable_outstanding.definition: Average number of days a company takes to pay its suppliers.
days_payable_outstanding.formula: DPO = (Accounts Payable / Cost of Goods Sold) × Number of Days
conventions: Number of Days = 365 for annual, 90 for quarterly.
Use ending accounts payable from balance sheet.
Use COGS from income statement for the same period.
Higher DPO = company takes longer to pay suppliers (retains cash longer).
days_payable_outstanding.where_to_find: Balance sheet (Accounts Payable) and Income statement (COGS)

days_sales_outstanding.name: Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)
days_sales_outstanding.definition: Average number of days it takes to collect payment after a sale.
days_sales_outstanding.formula: DSO = (Accounts Receivable / Total Revenue) × Number of Days
days_sales_outstanding.alternate_formula: DSO = (Accounts Receivable / Net Credit Sales) × Number of Days
conventions: Number of Days = 365 for annual, 90 for quarterly.
When net credit sales are not disclosed, use total revenue as proxy.
DSO below 45 days is generally considered good.
days_sales_outstanding.where_to_find: Balance sheet (Accounts Receivable) and Income statement (Revenue)

capital_expenditure.name: Capital Expenditure (CapEx)
capital_expenditure.definition: Funds used to acquire, upgrade, or maintain physical assets.
capital_expenditure.formula: CapEx = Purchases of Property, Plant and Equipment (from Cash Flow Statement)
capital_expenditure.alternate_formula: CapEx = Ending PP&E - Beginning PP&E + Depreciation
conventions: CapEx is found in the INVESTING ACTIVITIES section of the Cash Flow Statement.
The line item is typically 'Purchases of property, plant and equipment' or 'Capital expenditures'.
CapEx is reported as a NEGATIVE number on the cash flow statement (cash outflow). Use the ABSOLUTE VALUE when reporting.
Do NOT confuse with total investing activities, which includes acquisitions, divestitures, etc.
capital_expenditure.where_to_find: Cash Flow Statement — Investing Activities section

free_cash_flow.name: Free Cash Flow (FCF)
free_cash_flow.definition: Cash available after operating expenses and capital expenditures.
free_cash_flow.formula: FCF = Operating Cash Flow - Capital Expenditures
conventions: Operating Cash Flow = Net cash provided by operating activities.
CapEx should be the absolute value of purchases of PP&E.
FCF represents cash available for dividends, share buybacks, debt repayment, or investments.
Negative FCF is not always bad — could indicate heavy growth investment.
free_cash_flow.where_to_find: Cash Flow Statement — Operating Activities (net cash) minus Investing Activities (PP&E purchases)

asset_turnover.name: Asset Turnover Ratio
asset_turnover.definition: Measures how efficiently a company uses its assets to generate revenue.
asset_turnover.formula: Asset Turnover = Net Revenue / Average Total Assets
conventions: Average Total Assets = (Beginning Assets + Ending Assets) / 2.
Higher ratio = more efficient use of assets.
Varies significantly by industry.
asset_turnover.where_to_find: Income statement (Revenue) and Balance sheet (Total Assets)

price_to_earnings.name: Price-to-Earnings Ratio (P/E)
price_to_earnings.definition: Compares a company's stock price to its earnings per share.
price_to_earnings.formula: P/E Ratio = Stock Price / Earnings Per Share (EPS)
conventions: Trailing P/E uses actual EPS from the most recent 12 months.
Forward P/E uses projected EPS for the next 12 months.
EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Diluted Shares Outstanding.
price_to_earnings.where_to_find: Stock price (market data) and Income statement (EPS)

earnings_per_share.name: Earnings Per Share (EPS)
earnings_per_share.definition: Net income allocated to each outstanding share of common stock.
earnings_per_share.formula: Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding
earnings_per_share.alternate_formula: Diluted EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Diluted Shares Outstanding
conventions: Diluted EPS accounts for all potential shares from options, warrants, convertible securities.
Diluted EPS ≤ Basic EPS (more shares in denominator).
Both basic and diluted EPS are typically reported on the income statement.
earnings_per_share.where_to_find: Income statement — Earnings Per Share section (basic and diluted)

book_value_per_share.name: Book Value Per Share (BVPS)
book_value_per_share.definition: The net asset value of a company per outstanding share of common stock. In a liquidation scenario, this represents the theoretical payout per share.
book_value_per_share.formula: BVPS = (Total Shareholders' Equity - Preferred Equity) / Total Common Shares Outstanding
conventions: Subtract preferred stock from equity before dividing.
If no preferred stock exists, just divide total equity by shares outstanding.
BVPS > market price may suggest undervaluation.

For LIQUIDATION questions ('how much could each shareholder get'): use Total Stockholders' Equity from the balance sheet divided by common shares outstanding. This is the book value per share — the theoretical amount each common shareholder would receive if all assets were liquidated at book value and all liabilities paid.

For BANK / FINANCIAL INSTITUTION liquidation: use 'Total stockholders' equity' (NOT 'Total equity' which may include non-controlling interests) divided by common shares outstanding from the balance sheet face.

Shares outstanding: look on the balance sheet face (e.g., 'Common stock, shares outstanding: X,XXX,XXX') or in the equity section. Do NOT use diluted shares — use basic shares outstanding as reported on the balance sheet.
book_value_per_share.where_to_find: Balance sheet (Total Stockholders' Equity, Preferred Stock, Common Shares Outstanding)

interest_coverage_ratio.name: Interest Coverage Ratio (Times Interest Earned)
interest_coverage_ratio.definition: Measures how easily a company can pay interest on its outstanding debt.
interest_coverage_ratio.formula: Interest Coverage Ratio = EBIT / Interest Expense
interest_coverage_ratio.alternate_formula: Interest Coverage Ratio = EBITDA / Interest Expense
conventions: EBIT = Operating Income from the income statement. If the question asks for 'Adjusted EBIT', you must derive it from Adjusted EBITDA (if provided) minus D&A. Do NOT use EBITDAR as EBIT.
Interest Expense is found on the income statement (sometimes net of interest income).
Use gross interest expense, not net, unless the question specifies 'net interest'.
CRITICAL: If EBIT is NEGATIVE, the interest coverage ratio is ZERO — the company cannot cover interest. State this clearly: 'As adjusted EBIT is negative, the interest coverage ratio is zero.'
If the filing provides EBITDAR but NOT EBIT, you must subtract BOTH D&A AND Rent to get EBIT from EBITDAR. EBITDAR - D&A - Rent = EBIT.
Higher ratio = better ability to service debt. Below 1.5 is concerning.
interest_coverage_ratio.where_to_find: Income statement — Operating Income (EBIT) and Interest Expense

revenue_growth_rate.name: Revenue Growth Rate (Year-over-Year)
revenue_growth_rate.definition: The percentage change in revenue from one period to the next.
revenue_growth_rate.formula: Revenue Growth Rate = ((Current Period Revenue - Prior Period Revenue) / Prior Period Revenue) × 100
conventions: Compare equal periods (annual to annual, quarterly to quarterly).
A POSITIVE number means revenue increased; NEGATIVE means it declined.
'Organic growth' excludes the impact of acquisitions, divestitures, and currency effects.
'Top line' refers to total revenue (the first line of the income statement).
revenue_growth_rate.where_to_find: Income statement — Total Revenue / Net Sales for consecutive periods

capital_intensity.name: Capital Intensity
capital_intensity.definition: Measures how much capital (assets) a company needs to generate revenue.
capital_intensity.formula: Capital Intensity Ratio = Total Assets / Revenue
conventions: A company is capital-intensive if it requires substantial fixed assets (PP&E) relative to revenue.
Industries like utilities, telecom, airlines, oil & gas, healthcare/pharmacy are capital-intensive.
A high PP&E-to-revenue ratio OR high CapEx-to-revenue ratio indicates capital intensity.

CRITICAL: Low ROA (below 5%) is a STRONG indicator of capital intensity — it means the company needs a large asset base to generate modest profits. ALWAYS compute and cite ROA when assessing capital intensity.
Also consider: high depreciation expense relative to revenue, and large total asset base.
Note: for companies like CVS Health, a significant portion of assets may be goodwill/intangibles from acquisitions — mention this nuance but still conclude capital-intensive if ROA is low.
capital_intensity.where_to_find: Balance sheet (Total Assets, PP&E) and Income statement (Revenue) and Cash Flow Statement (CapEx)

depreciation_amortization_rate.name: Depreciation & Amortization as % of Revenue
depreciation_amortization_rate.definition: Depreciation and amortization expenses expressed as a percentage of total revenue.
depreciation_amortization_rate.formula: D&A % of Revenue = (Depreciation + Amortization) / Total Revenue × 100
conventions: When the question says 'D&A from the cash flow statement', use the D&A figure from the Operating Activities section of the Cash Flow Statement.
The correct D&A line is the one labeled 'Depreciation and amortization' in the cash flow statement adjustments to net income. This is the company's own PP&E depreciation + intangible amortization.
Do NOT include content amortization (streaming, film, music libraries), right-of-use asset amortization, or debt issuance cost amortization in this figure.
Revenue comes from the Income Statement (total revenue / net sales).
If D&A is not broken out on the income statement, the cash flow statement is the authoritative source.
depreciation_amortization_rate.where_to_find: Cash Flow Statement (D&A adjustment line in operating activities) and Income Statement (Total Revenue)

dividend_payout_ratio.name: Dividend Payout Ratio
dividend_payout_ratio.definition: The percentage of net income paid out as dividends to shareholders.
dividend_payout_ratio.formula: Dividend Payout Ratio = (Total Dividends Paid / Net Income) × 100
dividend_payout_ratio.alternate_formula: Dividend Payout Ratio = Dividends Per Share / Earnings Per Share × 100
conventions: Use dividends declared or dividends paid from the cash flow statement / equity section.
A payout ratio above 100% means the company is paying more in dividends than it earns (unsustainable long-term).
Stable or growing dividends over multiple years indicates a 'stable dividend policy'.
dividend_payout_ratio.where_to_find: Cash Flow Statement (dividends paid) or Equity section; Income Statement (net income)

notional_value.name: Notional Value (of Derivatives)
notional_value.definition: The total value of a leveraged position's assets, representing the underlying amount of a derivative contract.
notional_value.formula: Reported directly in the notes to financial statements — not calculated.
conventions: Notional value represents the total amount of the underlying asset.
The HIGHEST notional value = the derivative with the largest total contract amount.
Common derivatives: interest rate swaps, cross-currency swaps, foreign exchange forwards, commodity contracts.
Found in the Notes to Financial Statements under 'Derivative Instruments' or 'Fair Value Measurements'.
notional_value.where_to_find: Notes to Financial Statements — Derivative Instruments / Hedging Activities section

operating_cash_flow_ratio.name: Operating Cash Flow Ratio
operating_cash_flow_ratio.definition: Measures a company's ability to cover current liabilities with cash generated from operations.
operating_cash_flow_ratio.formula: Operating Cash Flow Ratio = Cash from Operations / Total Current Liabilities

numerator: Net cash provided by operating activities (from Cash Flow Statement)

conventions: Use 'Net cash provided by operating activities' from the Cash Flow Statement — this is the top-level operating cash flow number.
Use 'Total current liabilities' from the Balance Sheet as of the same period end.
A ratio above 1.0 means the company generates enough cash from operations to cover all current obligations.
Round to two decimal places unless otherwise specified.
operating_cash_flow_ratio.where_to_find: Cash Flow Statement (operating activities total) and Balance Sheet (total current liabilities)

restructuring_costs.name: Restructuring Costs / Charges
restructuring_costs.definition: One-time expenses associated with reorganizing a company's operations.
restructuring_costs.formula: Reported directly as a line item — not calculated.
conventions: May appear as a separate line item on the income statement OR embedded in operating expenses.
When the question asks what is 'directly outlined in the income statement', look for an explicit line item.
If not a separate line on the face of the income statement, check the Notes to Financial Statements for the breakdown.
The amount in the Notes may differ from the face of the income statement due to netting or classification.
restructuring_costs.where_to_find: Income statement (if separate line item) OR Notes to Financial Statements
